body:
Lockout-tagout procedure

Darren - never assume a circuit is dead. Test it.

  1. Identify the circuit at the panel. Confirm against the panel directory.
  2. Switch off breaker. Open disconnect if there is one.
  3. Apply lock and tag with your name and date.
  4. Verify dead. Check tester on a known-live source first, then the target, then the known-live source again.
  5. Only you remove your own lock. Nobody else touches it.

If anything reads live, stop and call me.
title: Lockout-tagout procedure

body:
Service panel upgrade - 200A SOP

Darren - this is a kill-you job if you rush it. Read it twice.

  1. Confirm PECO disconnect scheduled. No work on a hot service.
  2. Photograph existing panel and feeder before you touch anything.
  3. Cut and cap, set new 200A panel, land the grounding electrode conductor and bonding jumper.
  4. Move circuits over one at a time. Label as you go. Do not bundle neutrals and grounds on the same bar in a subfed setup.
  5. Torque every lug to spec. Mark each with a paint pen when checked.
  6. Call for rough then final inspection before PECO reconnect.
title: Service panel upgrade - 200A SOP

body:
Whole-house rewire - rough-in checklist

Darren - rough-in is where the money and the failures live. Slow down.

  • Boxes set to correct depth for the finished wall. Half inch max setback for combustible.
  • Home runs pulled to panel, labeled at both ends.
  • Drilled holes centered in studs, nail plates where wire is within 1-1/4 inch of the edge.
  • Staple within 8 inches of boxes, then every 4-1/2 feet.
  • Leave 6 inches of free conductor at each box.
  • Separate line voltage from low voltage.

Do not close walls until I sign off and inspector signs off.
title: Whole-house rewire - rough-in checklist

body:
GFCI and AFCI requirements cheat sheet

Darren - keep this in the van. Inspectors check it every time.

GFCI required: bathrooms, kitchens (counter receptacles), garages, outdoors, crawl spaces, unfinished basements, laundry, within 6 feet of a sink.

AFCI required: most habitable rooms - bedrooms, living rooms, dens, closets, hallways. Dual function breaker covers both.

When in doubt, dual function. Test every device before you leave with the test button and a plug-in tester.
title: GFCI and AFCI requirements cheat sheet

body:
Rough-in inspection prep

Darren - inspector should walk in and find nothing to write up. That is the goal.

  • All boxes installed, secured, no missing covers on junctions.
  • Conductors landed or made up where required, no open splices outside boxes.
  • Nail plates on at-risk runs.
  • Panel directory started, breakers labeled.
  • Grounding electrode and bonding visible and accessible.
  • Permit posted and visible from the street side.

Walk the whole job with the checklist the night before. Fix small stuff then, not while inspector is standing there.
title: Rough-in inspection prep

body:
Final inspection checklist

Darren - final is about devices, covers, and labels. Make it clean.

  • All devices installed, level, and tight. Cover plates on.
  • GFCI and AFCI tested and passing.
  • Panel directory filled out, legible, accurate.
  • Smoke and CO alarms installed and interconnected where required.
  • Fixtures hung, no exposed splices, no missing knockouts.
  • Bonding and grounding complete, ufer or rod connection accessible.

Bring the panel directory and permit card. Inspector signs the card, not your phone.
title: Final inspection checklist

body:
EV charger (Level 2) install steps

Darren - load calc first. Always. People want 60A on a 100A service and it does not fit.

  1. Run the load calculation. Confirm spare capacity before you quote.
  2. Set breaker per the unit nameplate. 48A unit needs a 60A circuit, 6 AWG copper.
  3. Mount unit per manufacturer height, usually 18 to 48 inches to the connector.
  4. GFCI protection per the unit. Many have it built in - do not double up unless allowed.
  5. Pull permit. Most townships want a separate EVSE permit now.
  6. Energize, test, walk the customer through the app pairing.
title: EV charger (Level 2) install steps

body:
Knob-and-tube replacement procedure

Darren - knob-and-tube is brittle and the insulation is gone. Treat every run as live until proven dead.

  1. Map what is on each old circuit. Old wiring crosses room boundaries in weird ways.
  2. De-energize and verify dead. Tag the panel.
  3. Do not splice new romex onto old K and T. Remove it back to a junction box or replace the whole run.
  4. Watch for insulation packed over K and T. That is a fire risk on its own.
  5. Re-ground. K and T has no equipment ground - new circuits need it.

If you find cloth wire taped into a fixture, stop and show me before you pull.
title: Knob-and-tube replacement procedure

body:
Meter swap coordination with PECO

Darren - we do not pull the meter on a whim. PECO owns the meter.

  1. Schedule the disconnect with PECO. Get the cut order number.
  2. Confirm the appointment window the day before.
  3. On the day, confirm the service is dead at the line side before work.
  4. Do our work. Have the inspection passed and the cut-in card ready.
  5. PECO reconnects and sets the meter. We do not set the meter ourselves.

Keep the cut order number in the job folder. They ask for it every time.
title: Meter swap coordination with PECO

body:
Ladder and fall safety

Darren - most of our injuries come off a ladder, not off a wire.

  • Inspect before every use. Cracked rail or loose rivet, it goes in the trash.
  • Fiberglass only near anything electrical. No aluminum.
  • Three points of contact going up and down. No tools in your hands on the climb - use the bucket and rope.
  • 4 to 1 angle on extension ladders. One foot out for every four feet up.
  • Extend 3 feet above the roof edge and tie off the top.
  • Do not stand on the top two rungs of a step ladder.
title: Ladder and fall safety

body:
PPE and arc-flash basics

Darren - the gear is not optional. I do not care if the job is two minutes.

  • Safety glasses on the whole time on site.
  • Insulated gloves rated for the work when there is any chance of contact. Inspect them for pinholes.
  • No rings, no watch, no metal bracelet when working in a panel.
  • Long sleeves, natural fiber or rated FR for panel work. No polyester near an arc.
  • Face shield and arc-rated gear for anything energized over 50 volts that we cannot kill.

If you cannot do it dead, we plan it as energized work and we both gear up.
title: PPE and arc-flash basics

body:
Wire sizing and ampacity quick reference

Darren - memorize the common ones. Copper, 60 to 75 degree C column, residential.

  • 14 AWG - 15A
  • 12 AWG - 20A
  • 10 AWG - 30A
  • 8 AWG - 40A
  • 6 AWG - 55A (use for 50A range and 48A EV)
  • 4 AWG - 70A
  • 2 AWG - 95A
  • 2/0 AWG aluminum - 150A feeder
  • 4/0 AWG aluminum - 200A service

Derate for heat, for bundling, and for continuous load. When unsure, go up a size.
title: Wire sizing and ampacity quick reference

body:
Troubleshooting a dead circuit

Darren - work it in order. Do not guess and start swapping parts.

  1. Confirm the complaint. What exactly is dead.
  2. Check the breaker. Tripped breakers do not always look tripped - push fully off then on.
  3. Check for a tripped GFCI upstream. One bad GFCI kills everything downstream.
  4. Open the first device on the run. Look for a backstabbed connection that let go.
  5. Voltage test at each point, source to load, until you find where it stops.
  6. Found it - fix it, retest, button up.

Half our dead circuits are a loose backstab or a tripped GFCI in another room.
title: Troubleshooting a dead circuit

body:
Drilling and fishing wire through walls

Darren - plan the path before you cut a single hole.

  • Locate studs and obstructions. Know where plumbing and existing wire run.
  • Drill centered through framing. Keep 1-1/4 inch from the edge or use a nail plate.
  • Use fish tape or glow rods. Tape the wire to the leader, do not just hook it.
  • Fish from the easy end toward the hard end.
  • Patch as small as you can. We own the drywall damage we make.

Do not drill blind into an exterior wall. Insulation, fire blocking, and brick ties live there.
title: Drilling and fishing wire through walls

body:
Grounding and bonding checklist

Darren - this is what keeps the customer alive when something faults. Get it right.

  • Grounding electrode conductor from panel to the rod or ufer, sized to the service.
  • Two ground rods 6 feet apart unless a single rod tests under 25 ohms.
  • Bond the metal water pipe within 5 feet of entry.
  • Bond gas piping where required.
  • Main bonding jumper at the service only. Neutral and ground separated in every subpanel.
  • Intersystem bonding bridge for cable and phone.
title: Grounding and bonding checklist

body:
Job-site setup and cleanup

Darren - how we leave a house is how the customer judges us. Clean job, clean reputation.

Setup:

  • Drop cloths down before you open a wall.
  • Stage tools on a tarp, not on the customer floor.
  • Protect the path from the door to the work area.

Cleanup:

  • Vacuum drywall dust, do not just sweep it around.
  • All scrap wire and packaging in the van, not their trash.
  • Wipe fingerprints off switch plates and walls.
  • Walk the job with the customer before we leave.
title: Job-site setup and cleanup

body:
Permit application steps (L&I)

Darren - no permit, no work on anything that needs one. This is the license on the line.

  1. Confirm jurisdiction. Philly is L and I. Suburbs go through the township or a third party inspector.
  2. Pull the electrical permit under the master license. Scope it accurately.
  3. Get the permit number into the job folder.
  4. Post the permit card on site, visible.
  5. Schedule rough then final. Do not cover work before rough passes.

If a customer pushes us to skip the permit, the answer is no. Bring it to me.
title: Permit application steps (L&I)

body:
Subpanel installation SOP

Darren - the one rule everybody breaks: separate the neutrals and grounds.

  1. Size the feeder and breaker to the load. 4-wire feeder, two hots, a neutral, a ground.
  2. In the subpanel, neutral bar floats - remove the bonding screw or strap.
  3. Ground bar is bonded to the can.
  4. Land neutrals on the neutral bar, grounds on the ground bar. Never share a hole.
  5. Feeder breaker at the main panel, not a sub-feed lug unless it is rated for it.
  6. Label the subpanel and update the main directory.
title: Subpanel installation SOP

body:
Reading a load calculation

Darren - the load calc tells you if the service can take the new load. Learn to read it.

  • General lighting load: 3 VA per square foot of living area.
  • Two small appliance circuits and one laundry circuit at 1500 VA each.
  • Apply the standard demand factors - first 10 kVA at 100 percent, remainder at 40 percent.
  • Add fixed appliances, heat or AC (the larger), range, dryer, water heater, EV.
  • Divide total VA by 240 to get amps. Compare to service rating.

If the math says the 100A service is full, the customer needs an upgrade before we add anything.
title: Reading a load calculation

body:
Tool and van inventory checklist

Darren - a stocked van is a profitable van. Check it Monday morning.

Hand tools: linesman pliers, side cutters, strippers, screwdrivers, nut drivers, voltage tester, multimeter, fish tape.

Power tools: drill, hole saws, recip saw, charged batteries.

Stock: 12 and 14 romex, single and double pole breakers, GFCI and AFCI breakers, devices, plates, wire nuts, staples, nail plates, boxes.

Safety: fiberglass ladder, gloves, glasses, locks and tags, fire extinguisher.

Anything you use up, write it on the board so we restock.
title: Tool and van inventory checklist

body:
Customer walkthrough and sign-off

Darren - the walkthrough closes the job. Do not rush the customer out the door.

  1. Show every device and fixture you installed. Prove it works in front of them.
  2. Walk them through the new panel directory.
  3. Point out the GFCI test buttons and how to reset.
  4. Explain any warranty on parts.
  5. Get the sign-off and a photo of the finished work for our file.
  6. Thank them, hand them the card, leave it cleaner than we found it.
title: Customer walkthrough and sign-off

body:
Winter / cold-weather job-site safety

Darren - Philly winters wreck job sites. Plan for it.

  • Watch for ice on ladders and roofs. If it is sketchy, we reschedule.
  • Cold makes romex stiff and the jacket cracks. Keep it warm in the van until you pull it.
  • Salt and slush track in - extra drop cloths and mats.
  • Keep batteries warm. Cold kills runtime fast.
  • Frozen ground means the rod gets driven before the freeze or we use a chemical electrode.
  • Layer up but no loose scarves or strings near the panel.
title: Winter / cold-weather job-site safety

body:
Apprentice first-90-days checklist

Darren - this is where you should be by the end of the first stretch. We work through it together.

  • Lockout-tagout every time, no shortcuts.
  • Strip and make up a clean device without nicking conductors.
  • Identify breaker, wire, and box types on sight.
  • Read the panel and trace a circuit.
  • Drive the van stocked and keep the inventory board current.
  • Talk to a customer without committing us to anything. When unsure, say you will check with me.
title: Apprentice first-90-days checklist

body:
Multimeter and tester use

Darren - your tester is your life insurance. Learn it cold.

  • Verify the tester on a known-live source before and after every dead check.
  • Voltage mode for live readings - red to the hot, black to neutral or ground.
  • Continuity mode only on a dead, isolated circuit. Never on a live one.
  • Resistance and continuity tell you if a wire is broken or a splice is open.
  • A non-contact tester is a first pass, not proof. Always confirm with the meter.

If the reading does not make sense, stop and recheck your leads before you trust it.
title: Multimeter and tester use
